howItWorks.intro: This social security benefit calculator uses established formulas to provide accurate results.

rules.name: PIA Formula
rules.explanation: The progressive benefit formula replaces a higher percentage of lower earnings.

rules.name: Delayed Credits
rules.formula: 8% increase per year past FRA (up to age 70)
rules.explanation: Each year you delay past full retirement age adds 8% to your benefit permanently.

howItWorks.outro: Tax laws and financial markets change frequently. Verify current rates with your financial institution.

faq.q: Should I claim Social Security at 62 or wait?
faq.a: Claiming at 62 gives you smaller checks for more years. Waiting until 70 gives you roughly 76% more per month than at 62. If you expect to live past 80, delaying usually pays more total. If you need income now or have health concerns, claiming earlier may make sense.

SSA takes your highest 35 years of inflation-adjusted earnings, divides by 420 months to get AIME (Average Indexed Monthly Earnings), then applies a progressive formula: 90% of the first $1,174, 32% of AIME between $1,174-$7,078, and 15% above $7,078 (2024 bend points).

faq.q: Can I work while collecting Social Security?
faq.a: Yes, but before full retirement age, benefits are reduced by $1 for every $2 earned above $22,320 (2024). After FRA there is no earnings penalty, and your benefit may be recalculated higher.
